hints.nominative: Nominative masculine plural would be 'ei/ii' — 'Eorum' with -orum is genitive plural
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'eis/iis' — 'Eorum' is genitive plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'eis/iis' — 'Eorum' is genitive plural
While 'una' can be ablative feminine singular, here it modifies 'pars' as nominative
hints.accusative: Accusative feminine would be 'unam' — 'una' here is nominative modifying 'pars'
hints.vocative: Vocative would be the same form, but Caesar is describing, not addressing

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'partis' — 'pars' is nominative, subject of 'attingit'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'partem' — 'pars' is nominative here
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'parte' — 'pars' is nominative, subject of the sentence

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'partis' ends in -is, marking 3rd declension
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'partis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'partis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: While 'flumen' looks the same in nominative, here it is the object of 'attingit'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'flumine' — 'flumen' is accusative, direct object of 'attingit'
hints.vocative: Vocative is not used here — 'flumen' is accusative, object of the verb

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'fluminis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'fluminis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'fluminis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Rhenus' — 'Rhenum' with -um is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Rheni' — 'Rhenum' is accusative, in apposition with 'flumen'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Rheno' — 'Rhenum' with -um is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Rheni' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Rheni' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Rheni' with -i is 2nd declension

hints.to cross: That would be 'transire' — 'attingit' comes from 'attingere' (to reach/touch)
hints.to flow: That would be 'fluere' — 'attingit' comes from 'attingere' (to reach/touch)
hints.to divide: That would be 'dividere' — 'attingit' comes from 'attingere' (to reach/touch)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'attigit' — 'attingit' with the nasal infix -ng- is present
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'attinget' — 'attingit' with -it is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'attingat' — 'attingit' is present indicative

hints.tango, tangere, tetigi, tactum: These are the parts of 'tangere' (to touch) — 'attingere' is its compound ad + tangere
hints.tingo, tingere, tinxi, tinctum: These are the parts of 'tingere' (to dye/wet)
hints.cingo, cingere, cinxi, cinctum: These are the parts of 'cingere' (to surround)

translation: One part of them reaches the river Rhine
